text: The surgeon clears the retromuscular space on the patient's right side, identifying the linea alba and dissecting down towards the arcuate line. The arcuate line is incised to expose the prominent preperitoneal fat pad, which is dissected down to the pubis. The contralateral sheath is identified and incised to enter the contralateral retromuscular plane, performing a lower crossover approach. Branches off the epigastrics are encountered, and careful dissection is performed to maintain visualization despite a small hole in the peritoneum. The dissection is extended to the contralateral semilunar line, using heat to keep the field bloodless.

The surgeon performs a lower crossover dissection below the arcuate line, carefully distinguishing between the peritoneum and preperitoneal fat. The epigastric vessels are mobilized away from the midline. The posterior sheath is incised, revealing adhesions that prevent standard pneumodissection. Multiple preperitoneal hernias are identified, including a larger fat-containing hernia not seen on CT scan. The bedside assistant applies gentle pressure to aid in reducing the smaller hernia. Progress is made towards completing the upper crossover, with sufficient space exposed to work on the contralateral side.

The surgeon opts to proceed with an upper crossover, incising the contralateral sheath and identifying the rectus muscle to develop the retromuscular space cephalad. The robust muscular anatomy, including the transversus abdominis, is visible. Despite challenges in reducing the incarcerated hernia, an incision is made to facilitate the reduction of the transverse colon and omentum, followed by closure of the posterior sheath, placement of mesh, and closure of the anterior sheath.
